cards.title: What the Facebook Pixel actually is
cards.front: Everyone says "install the Pixel" — but what IS the thing you're pasting onto your site?
cards.back: The Pixel is a JavaScript block placed on your site. The block is IDENTICAL for every advertiser — the only unique part is your Facebook Pixel ID, a number unique to your ad account that tells Facebook which account the activity belongs to. This common block is called the Base Pixel / Base Pixel Code, and it's the foundation of all data you send to Facebook.
cards.example: Replace {{YourFBPIXELID}} in the snippet with your numeric Pixel ID — that's the only edit you make.

cards.title: The Pixel is not tracking-only
cards.front: Most advertisers think the Pixel is just a tracking tag. What are they missing?
cards.back: Treating the Pixel as tracking-only underuses TWO of its three super powers: audience building and optimization. Facebook's official definition calls it "an analytics tool that allows you to measure the effectiveness of your advertising by understanding the actions people take on your website" — but every Pixel decision should serve all three jobs, not just measurement.
cards.example: Guiding quote, Claude Hopkins (Scientific Advertising): "Never be guided in any way by ads that are untracked."

cards.title: Three Super Powers of the Pixel
cards.front: Beyond tracking, what are the three jobs a properly-used Pixel does for you?
cards.back:
The Three Super Powers of the Facebook Pixel:
- Audience Building — "Reach the right people." Build audiences from on-site behavior so ads show to the right people.
- Optimization — "Drive more sales." Unlock Facebook tools and let the algorithm find more people likely to convert.
- Tracking & Measurement — "Measure the results of your ads." Understand the actions people take on your site.

cards.title: Install the Pixel on every page
cards.front: Which pages of your site need the Pixel installed?
cards.back: Install the Pixel on EVERY single page — home page, blog/article pages, sales pages, add-to-cart pages, checkout pages, and everything in between. Website visitor activity is the most common audience-building behavior, so tracking all of it lets you build custom audiences for retargeting/remarketing. The benchmark is 100% of pages.
cards.example: Place the pixel on any page you want to measure results from, then build retargeting audiences from that traffic.

cards.title: Flying blind without a configured Pixel
cards.front: What happens to Facebook's algorithm if your Pixel isn't properly configured?
cards.back: Without the Pixel set up for proper conversion tracking, you won't know what's working — you're flying blind. Facebook's algorithms only do the heavy lifting (finding more converters) if the Pixel is set up and configured properly. No configured Pixel = no algorithmic help.

cards.title: Events vs Standard Events
cards.front: PageView fires automatically — but what exactly is an "event" and what makes one "standard"?
cards.back: An Event is an on-site action you want Facebook to know about (PageView is one). Events are used to build audiences, track conversions, and help Facebook learn WHO takes desired actions so it can target similar people. The Pixel works with a predefined set of common actions called Standard Events (full list in Facebook's Advertising Help section).

cards.title: What the Base Code does, step by step
cards.front: When the Base Pixel loads, what four things actually happen behind the scenes?
cards.back:
- Notifies Facebook's Ad Tracking servers there's an active visitor and requests the functions to send data.
- Facebook responds with functions — most importantly fbq, used to send data in a language Facebook understands.
- fbq('init', '{{YourFBPIXELID}}'); initializes the pixel, associating all page activity with your Pixel ID for correct attribution.
- fbq('track', 'PageView'); registers the first event. Behind the scenes it sends the page URL, the visitor's unique Facebook User ID, a timestamp, and helper data — so Facebook knows WHO is on the site, WHAT page, the Pixel ID, and the related ad account.

cards.title: The 5 most common Standard Events
cards.front: Which Standard Events should you actually be firing in a typical funnel?
cards.back:
Most common Standard Events:
- Lead — someone opts in to your email list / follow-up channel
- Purchase — fired when a purchase occurs (the only event with mandatory parameters)
- Complete Registration — webinar registrations, event registrations, etc.
- Add To Cart (AddToCart) — visitor adds a product to the cart
- Initiate Checkout (InitiateCheckout) — visitor reaches the checkout form before purchasing

cards.title: Base Pixel Code template
cards.front: What's the exact code block to paste in your <head> — and the one edit you must make?
cards.back:
Place this in <head>...</head> so it runs before any event code. Replace {{YourFBPIXELID}} with your numeric Pixel ID:

fbq('init', '{{YourFBPIXELID}}'); // Insert your pixel ID here.
fbq('track', 'PageView');

The full block loads fbevents.js from connect.facebook.net, includes a <noscript> tracking pixel fallback (img to facebook.com/tr?id=...&ev=PageView&noscript=1), and is marked DO NOT MODIFY. Only the Pixel ID changes.

cards.title: Standard Event code template
cards.front: Once the base code is loaded, how do you fire a simple Standard Event like Lead?
cards.example: Fire fbq('track', 'CompleteRegistration'); on your webinar thank-you page.

cards.title: Order: Base Code before event code
cards.front: Your event isn't firing — what's the most common source-order mistake?
cards.back: Fire the Base Pixel Code on ALL pages, on page load, in the <head> so it runs completely before any event code. Event code must come AFTER the Base Pixel Code in top-to-bottom page-source order, so fbq is loaded before the event fires. Typical pattern: Base Pixel in <head>, event code in <body>. If an event runs before fbq is loaded, it won't fire properly.

cards.title: Don't nest event code in the Base block
cards.front: Should event code go inside the Base Pixel block? A common misconception says yes.
cards.back: No. Putting event code inside the Base Pixel block is a common misconception — it's unnecessary and often makes implementation harder. Event code can fire almost anywhere on the page, as long as it's after the base code. Don't trap it inside the base block.

cards.title: Facebook doesn't enforce event names
cards.front: If you fire AddToCart on a "Contact Us" page, will Facebook flag it?
cards.back: No. Aside from Purchase, Facebook doesn't know or care when/how you fire events — the names carry no special meaning. Firing AddToCart on a "Contact Us" page won't be flagged, but it WILL confuse your reporting. Always fire each Standard Event with the right contextual meaning in the right circumstances so you don't confuse yourself or your team.

cards.title: Find your Base Pixel Code in Business Manager
cards.front: Where do you actually grab your Base Pixel Code inside Business Manager?
cards.back:
- Go to Business Manager → main menu (top-left) → Pixels under the Assets submenu
- New ad account: follow the setup wizard prompts; the Base Pixel Code is provided at the end (do this ASAP after creating the account)
- Existing ad account: on the Pixels screen click Actions → View Pixel Code, then read it under "1 Install Pixel Base Code"

cards.title: Purchase is the only event with required params
cards.front: Which Standard Event forces you to include parameters — and exactly which two?
cards.back: Purchase is the ONLY event with mandatory parameters: value and currency (2 required parameters). Facebook wants to know how much the purchase was worth. Parameters are optional for all other events. Example verbatim code uses value: 54.00 and currency: 'USD'.

cards.title: Purchase event with parameters
cards.front: What does a correctly-parameterized Purchase event look like in code?

cards.title: Parameters: descriptors that add a payload
cards.front: What are parameters, and how granular can they make your audiences?
cards.back: Parameters add descriptors (a payload) to an event, telling Facebook more about it. They are open-ended — add as many as you want and name them whatever you like. Use them in Business Manager to build highly granular Custom Audiences or Custom Conversions. Important limit: only parameters that have already had traffic on your site appear in the pre-populated dropdowns.

cards.title: Key rules about parameters
cards.front: What are the five rules you must remember about Pixel parameters?
cards.back:
- Mandatory ONLY on the Purchase event (currency and value); optional for all others
- Completely open-ended — add and name as many as you want
- Use them in Business Manager to build granular Custom Audiences / Custom Conversions
- Only parameters that already had site activity appear in the pre-populated dropdowns
- Consult Facebook's Advertising Help section for the full list of events and their commonly used / mandatory parameters

cards.title: Three Ways Standard Events Are Used
cards.front: A single Standard Event feeds all three super powers — how does each one work?
cards.back:
Three Ways Standard Events Are Used:
- Tracking & Measurement — Standard Events align with the columns/metrics in Ads Manager for reporting.
- Audience Construction — In Audiences, create a Custom Audience based on Website Traffic, choose Custom Combination, switch the dropdown from URL to Event, and specify which events include/exclude people (e.g., fired AddToCart but not Purchase).
- Optimization — In a website conversion campaign at the ad set level, designate an Optimization Event (usually a Standard Event). You tell Facebook: "look for people who do this event, then find more people similar to them." Its Machine Learning then finds the similar people and raises the odds your ad reaches them.

cards.title: Off-objective conversions still track
cards.front: You optimized for Leads — will a Purchase that happens anyway still show up in reporting?
cards.back: Yes. Off-objective conversions still track. A conversion different from your chosen optimization event is still recorded in reporting — as long as you created a Standard Event or a Custom Conversion for it. Trackable actions include number of leads, cost per lead, purchase data, cart/checkout activity, visitor engagement, and button clicks. After receiving data, Facebook attributes each measurable action to the ad that triggered it.

cards.title: Track all conversions on Like campaigns
cards.front: Why bother tracking conversions on a simple Like ad?
cards.back: At the bottom of a Like ad, choose to track "All conversions from my Facebook pixel." It's worth doing because someone might click Like and then opt in to a lead gen ad a week later — and your Like campaign will get credit for that downstream conversion.

cards.title: Verify with the Facebook Pixel Helper
cards.front: How do you confirm the Pixel actually fires correctly across your whole funnel?
cards.back:
Use the Facebook Pixel Helper (Chrome extension):
- Install the Pixel Helper on Chrome
- Click each ad's URL and confirm the pixel fires on every page of the funnel
- Cross-reference the reported Pixel ID against your Ad Account Pixel ID in Ads Manager → All Tools → Pixels
- Confirm the IDs match on every page of the funnel
